model_narrative: We distribute bagged fertilizer across Morocco from a set of bagging centers to every market center, over a network of cities connected by rail and road. For each bagging center we decide how to route its product through the network: how much to load onto the network at the center, which arcs to send flow along and in which mode, where and how much to switch transport mode along the way, and how much to unload at the markets. Loading, unloading, switching modes, and traveling each kilometer all carry a per-ton cost that depends on the mode. The objective is to minimize the total transport cost per ton.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: all nodes of the rail and road network, including bagging centers, market centers, and intermediate junctions

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the available transport modes

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the source nodes, that is the bagging centers from which product originates; flows are tracked separately for each source

sets.name: d
sets.doc: the destination nodes, that is the market centers that must each receive product

params.name: darcs
params.doc: the directed arc length in kilometers from one node to another in a given mode; an entry of zero means there is no usable arc between those two nodes in that mode, so a positive value is what marks an arc as existing. A node has a mode available exactly when it appears on at least one arc of positive length in that mode.

params.name: mrate
params.doc: the travel cost in dollars per kilometer per ton for each mode

params.name: lrate
params.doc: the loading cost in dollars per ton for each mode

params.name: urate
params.doc: the unloading cost in dollars per ton for each mode

params.name: srate
params.doc: the cost in dollars per ton of switching from the first mode to the second mode at a node; switching to the same mode is free

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the flow originating at a given source that travels along a directed arc from one node to another in a given mode

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the amount loaded at a source in a given mode; loading injects product into the network at the source node

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the amount originating at a given source that is unloaded at a node in a given mode; unloading removes product from the network at a market center

vars.name: w
vars.doc: the amount originating at a given source that switches from the first mode to the second mode at a node

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: the total transport cost per ton

vars.name: phil
vars.doc: the total loading cost per ton

vars.name: phiu
vars.doc: the total unloading cost per ton

vars.name: phis
vars.doc: the total mode-switching cost per ton

vars.name: phim
vars.doc: the total per-kilometer travel cost per ton

constraints.name: darcs
constraints.intent: At every node that the network actually reaches in a given mode, flow must be conserved separately for each source. For each source and for each such node and mode, the product arriving at the node must be at least as much as the product leaving it. Product arrives at the node by flowing in along the arcs that lead into it, by switching into this mode from another mode at the node, and, when the node is the source itself, by being loaded onto the network there. Product leaves the node by flowing out along the arcs that lead away from it, by switching out of this mode to another mode at the node, and, when the node is a market center, by being unloaded there. Only consider nodes and modes that the network actually reaches.

constraints.name: darcs:
constraints.intent: Every market center must receive product from each source. For each source and each market center, the total amount unloaded at that market across the transport modes available there must be at least one unit.

constraints.name: darcs:
constraints.intent: Define the total loading cost. Set the loading cost equal to the amount loaded at each source in each mode available at that source, priced at that mode's loading rate, summed over all sources and modes.

constraints.name: darcs:
constraints.intent: Define the total unloading cost. Set the unloading cost equal to the amount unloaded at each market center in each mode available there, priced at that mode's unloading rate, summed over all sources and markets.

constraints.name: darcs:
constraints.intent: Define the total mode-switching cost. Set the switching cost equal to the amount switched from one mode to another at each node, priced at the switching rate for that ordered pair of modes, summed over all sources, all nodes, and every ordered pair of modes available at the node.

constraints.name: phim
constraints.intent: Define the total travel cost. Set the travel cost equal to the flow sent along each usable arc in its mode, multiplied by the arc's distance and by that mode's per-kilometer travel rate, summed over all sources and all usable arcs.

constraints.name: phi
constraints.intent: Define the total transport cost. Set the total cost equal to the sum of the loading cost, the unloading cost, the switching cost, and the travel cost.

query: Rail capacity might be tighter next season. We're looking at holding total rail loading across all the bagging centers to no more than 120 tons — what does the optimizer return for total cost under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.rail_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[s, 'rail'] for s in model.s) <= 120.0)

query: Our road carrier wants a minimum-volume commitment. Suppose we guarantee at least 60 tons of total road loading across the centers — what's the cost picture the model gives back?
expected_pyomo: model.road_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[s, 'road'] for s in model.s) >= 60.0)

query: The transfer yards are getting congested. We want to see what happens if we limit the total amount of cargo switched between rail and road network-wide to 50 tons — what total cost comes out?

query: All this back-and-forth between rail and road looks wasteful. Couldn't we just hold total mode-switching across the network to 60 tons and be done with it? What's keeping the model from doing fewer transfers on its own?
expected_pyomo: model.switch_floor_rule = Constraint(expr=sum(model.w[idx] for idx in model.w) <= 60.0)

query: We're carrying a lot of tonnage on rail overall. Shouldn't we cap total rail loading at 100 tons to keep the rail bill in check? What's the model seeing that makes it want to load that much onto rail?
expected_pyomo: model.rail_total_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[s, 'rail'] for s in model.s) <= 100.0)
